instruction: Explain V8's hidden classes and how they affect JavaScript performance. How should I structure objects for optimal V8 optimization?
output:
<think>
V8's hidden classes (also called "maps" or "shapes" internally) are the foundation of V8's object property access optimization. Instead of using hash table lookups for every property access (which is what a naive JS engine would do), V8 creates hidden class hierarchies that enable inline caching — property access becomes a direct memory offset read, like a C struct field access.

When you create an object `{x: 1, y: 2}`, V8 creates a chain of hidden classes: empty → {x at offset 0} → {x at offset 0, y at offset 8}. Objects with the same property names added in the same order share the same hidden class chain.

This matters because V8's JIT compiler (TurboFan) generates optimized code based on the hidden class. If a function always receives objects with the same hidden class, property access compiles to a single memory load with a guard check. This is called a "monomorphic" call site.

Problems that break optimization:
1. Adding properties in different orders → different hidden classes → polymorphic/megamorphic
2. Deleting properties → forces transition to "slow mode" (dictionary/hash table)
3. Adding too many properties dynamically → transitions overflow
4. Mixing types in the same property across instances → can't specialize

I should show concrete examples of good vs bad patterns, and how to verify with V8 flags like --trace-maps and --trace-ic.

Also important: arrays. V8 has special fast paths for arrays with contiguous integer indices ("packed" arrays). Holes in arrays (sparse arrays) force a slower representation. And mixing types in arrays (SMI → double → object) causes transitions.
</think>

Here's how V8's hidden classes work and how to write optimization-friendly JavaScript:

```javascript
// ============================================================
// 1. Hidden Class Basics
// ============================================================

// GOOD: Same property order → same hidden class → fast
function createPointGood(x, y) {
  const p = {};  // Hidden class: C0 (empty)
  p.x = x;       // Transition: C0 → C1 {x: offset 0}
  p.y = y;       // Transition: C1 → C2 {x: offset 0, y: offset 8}
  return p;
}

// All points share hidden class C2 → monomorphic access
const p1 = createPointGood(1, 2);
const p2 = createPointGood(3, 4);
const p3 = createPointGood(5, 6);
// p1, p2, p3 all have the SAME hidden class

// BAD: Different property order → different hidden classes
function createPointBad(x, y, swap) {
  const p = {};  // C0
  if (swap) {
    p.y = y;     // C0 → C3 {y: offset 0}
    p.x = x;     // C3 → C4 {y: offset 0, x: offset 8}
  } else {
    p.x = x;     // C0 → C1 {x: offset 0}
    p.y = y;     // C1 → C2 {x: offset 0, y: offset 8}
  }
  return p;
}
// C2 ≠ C4! Even though both have x and y, offsets differ.

// ============================================================
// 2. Impact on function optimization (IC states)
// ============================================================

// Monomorphic: always sees ONE hidden class → fastest
function distanceMono(p) {
  return Math.sqrt(p.x * p.x + p.y * p.y);
  // V8 compiles p.x as: load [p + 0] (direct offset)
}

// Polymorphic: sees 2-4 hidden classes → slower (checks each)
function distancePoly(p) {
  return Math.sqrt(p.x * p.x + p.y * p.y);
  // V8 generates: if (map == C2) load [p+0] else if (map == C4) load [p+8] ...
}

// Megamorphic: sees 5+ hidden classes → slowest (hash table lookup)
function distanceMega(p) {
  return Math.sqrt(p.x * p.x + p.y * p.y);
  // V8 falls back to generic property lookup
}

// ============================================================
// 3. Benchmark: hidden class impact
// ============================================================

function benchmarkHiddenClasses() {
  const N = 1_000_000;

  // Create monomorphic array (all same hidden class)
  const monoPoints = [];
  for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {
    monoPoints.push({ x: i, y: i + 1 }); // Always same order
  }

  // Create polymorphic array (multiple hidden classes)
  const polyPoints = [];
  for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {
    if (i % 2 === 0) {
      polyPoints.push({ x: i, y: i + 1 });         // HC1
    } else {
      polyPoints.push({ x: i, y: i + 1, z: 0 });   // HC2
    }
  }

  function sumX(points) {
    let sum = 0;
    for (let i = 0; i < points.length; i++) {
      sum += points[i].x;
    }
    return sum;
  }

  // Warm up
  sumX(monoPoints);
  sumX(polyPoints);

  console.time('Monomorphic');
  for (let r = 0; r < 10; r++) sumX(monoPoints);
  console.timeEnd('Monomorphic');

  console.time('Polymorphic');
  for (let r = 0; r < 10; r++) sumX(polyPoints);
  console.timeEnd('Polymorphic');
  // Monomorphic is typically 2-5x faster
}

// ============================================================
// 4. Rules for hidden class optimization
// ============================================================

// RULE 1: Always initialize properties in the same order
class Point {
  constructor(x, y) {
    // All instances get the same hidden class
    this.x = x;
    this.y = y;
  }
}

// RULE 2: Initialize all properties in constructor (don't add later)
class BadEntity {
  constructor(id) {
    this.id = id;
    // hp and mp added later → hidden class transitions
  }
  init() {
    this.hp = 100; // Transition!
    this.mp = 50;  // Another transition!
  }
}

class GoodEntity {
  constructor(id) {
    this.id = id;
    this.hp = 0;   // Initialize everything upfront
    this.mp = 0;
  }
  init() {
    this.hp = 100; // Same hidden class — just value change
    this.mp = 50;
  }
}

// RULE 3: Never delete properties
function badDelete(obj) {
  delete obj.temp; // Forces "slow mode" (dictionary) — NEVER recovers
}

function goodCleanup(obj) {
  obj.temp = undefined; // Same hidden class, just null value
}

// RULE 4: Use classes or consistent object literals
// BAD: factory functions with conditional properties
function createUser(name, isAdmin) {
  const user = { name };
  if (isAdmin) user.permissions = ['all']; // Different hidden class!
  return user;
}

// GOOD: always include all properties
function createUserGood(name, isAdmin) {
  return {
    name,
    permissions: isAdmin ? ['all'] : null,
  };
}

// ============================================================
// 5. Array optimization: packed vs holey
// ============================================================

// PACKED_SMI (fastest): contiguous small integers
const packed = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5];

// PACKED_DOUBLE: contiguous numbers (including floats)
const doubles = [1.1, 2.2, 3.3];

// PACKED_ELEMENTS: contiguous mixed types
const mixed = [1, 'hello', {}];

// HOLEY (slow): has gaps
const holey = [1, , 3]; // Hole at index 1!
const alsoHoley = new Array(1000); // All holes!

// RULE: Prefer Array.from() or push() over new Array(n)
const good = Array.from({ length: 1000 }, (_, i) => i);

// Verify with: node --allow-natives-syntax -e "
//   const a = [1,2,3];
//   %DebugPrint(a);  // Shows elements kind
// "

benchmarkHiddenClasses();
```

Key rules: always initialize properties in the same order (use classes), never delete properties, include all properties upfront even if null, keep arrays contiguous and same-typed. Verify with `node --trace-maps` or `--trace-ic` flags.

instruction: What are monomorphic call sites in V8 and why do they matter? How do I keep my JavaScript functions monomorphic?
output:
<think>
Monomorphic call sites are the most important V8 optimization concept for function-level performance. V8 uses inline caches (ICs) at each property access and function call site to specialize based on the types it observes at runtime.

The IC states are:
1. Uninitialized — never executed
2. Monomorphic — always sees the same type/hidden class → generates specialized code
3. Polymorphic (2-4 types) — generates a lookup chain with guards for each type
4. Megamorphic (5+ types) — falls back to generic dictionary lookup

Once a call site becomes megamorphic, it's very hard to recover. TurboFan (the optimizing compiler) can still optimize polymorphic sites with type guards, but megamorphic sites stay slow.

Common ways to break monomorphism:
1. Passing different object shapes to the same function
2. Using the same function for different numeric types (SMI vs HeapNumber vs Double)
3. Optional parameters that change the argument count
4. Functions that handle multiple return types

The key insight: it's the CALL SITE that matters, not the function itself. The same function can be monomorphic at one call site and megamorphic at another.

I should show practical examples: processing arrays of uniform vs mixed objects, the reduce pattern trap, and how to split polymorphic functions into monomorphic specializations.

Also worth noting: V8's Maglev compiler (Go 1.8+) is faster at compiling than TurboFan and handles polymorphic cases better, but monomorphic is still king for peak performance.
</think>

Here's how inline cache states work and how to keep functions monomorphic:

```javascript
// ============================================================
// 1. Inline Cache (IC) States
// ============================================================
//
// At each property access/function call, V8 maintains an IC:
//
//   UNINITIALIZED → MONOMORPHIC → POLYMORPHIC → MEGAMORPHIC
//   (never run)     (1 shape)     (2-4 shapes)  (5+ shapes)
//
// Monomorphic: property access compiles to a single memory load
// Polymorphic: generates a chain of type checks
// Megamorphic: falls back to hash table lookup (10-100x slower)

// ============================================================
// 2. Demonstration: mono vs poly vs mega
// ============================================================

// Shape A: { x, y }
// Shape B: { x, y, z }
// Shape C: { a, b }
// Shape D: { x, y, name }
// Shape E: { x, y, name, id }
// Shape F: { x, y, color }

function getX(obj) {
  return obj.x;  // This is the call site with an IC
}

// Monomorphic usage (fast):
function monoExample() {
  const points = [];
  for (let i = 0; i < 100000; i++) {
    points.push({ x: i, y: i });  // All same shape
  }
  let sum = 0;
  for (const p of points) {
    sum += getX(p);  // IC stays monomorphic
  }
  return sum;
}

// Megamorphic usage (slow):
function megaExample() {
  const objects = [];
  for (let i = 0; i < 100000; i++) {
    // 5+ different shapes → megamorphic
    switch (i % 5) {
      case 0: objects.push({ x: i, y: i }); break;
      case 1: objects.push({ x: i, y: i, z: i }); break;
      case 2: objects.push({ x: i, name: 'a' }); break;
      case 3: objects.push({ x: i, id: i, name: 'b' }); break;
      case 4: objects.push({ x: i, color: 'red', size: 1 }); break;
    }
  }
  let sum = 0;
  for (const o of objects) {
    sum += getX(o);  // IC is megamorphic → hash table lookup each time
  }
  return sum;
}

// ============================================================
// 3. The CALL SITE matters, not the function
// ============================================================

function add(a, b) { return a + b; }

// Call site 1: always numbers → monomorphic for number addition
function numericWork() {
  let sum = 0;
  for (let i = 0; i < 1000000; i++) {
    sum = add(sum, i);  // Monomorphic: number + number
  }
  return sum;
}

// Call site 2: always strings → monomorphic for string concatenation
function stringWork() {
  let result = '';
  for (let i = 0; i < 1000; i++) {
    result = add(result, 'x');  // Monomorphic: string + string
  }
  return result;
}

// Both call sites are monomorphic even though they use the same function!
// V8 optimizes each call site independently.

// BUT if you mix types at ONE call site:
function mixedWork(arr) {
  let result = 0;
  for (const item of arr) {
    result = add(result, item);  // Polymorphic: number + number OR number + string
  }
  return result;
}

// ============================================================
// 4. Common polymorphism traps
// ============================================================

// TRAP 1: Array.reduce with mixed initial values
const numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5];

// Polymorphic: first iteration is (0, 1), rest are (sum, n)
// But V8 handles this well for simple primitives.
// The real trap is with objects:

// BAD: reducer sees different object shapes
const items = [
  { type: 'a', value: 1 },
  { type: 'b', value: 2, extra: true },  // Different shape!
];
const total = items.reduce((sum, item) => sum + item.value, 0);

// TRAP 2: Config objects with optional fields
function processConfig(config) {
  const timeout = config.timeout || 5000;
  const retries = config.retries || 3;
  return { timeout, retries };
}

// Each call creates a different hidden class for config:
processConfig({ timeout: 1000 });                    // Shape A
processConfig({ timeout: 2000, retries: 5 });         // Shape B
processConfig({ timeout: 3000, retries: 1, log: true }); // Shape C
// processConfig's IC for config.timeout is now polymorphic

// FIX: Normalize config objects
function normalizeConfig(config) {
  return {
    timeout: config.timeout ?? 5000,
    retries: config.retries ?? 3,
    log: config.log ?? false,
  };
}

function processConfigGood(rawConfig) {
  const config = normalizeConfig(rawConfig);
  // config always has the same shape → monomorphic
  return { timeout: config.timeout, retries: config.retries };
}

// ============================================================
// 5. Splitting polymorphic functions
// ============================================================

// BAD: one function handles multiple types → polymorphic IC
function serialize(value) {
  if (typeof value === 'string') return `"${value}"`;
  if (typeof value === 'number') return String(value);
  if (Array.isArray(value)) return `[${value.map(serialize).join(',')}]`;
  return JSON.stringify(value);
}

// GOOD: separate functions per type → each stays monomorphic
function serializeString(value) { return `"${value}"`; }
function serializeNumber(value) { return String(value); }
function serializeArray(value) {
  const parts = [];
  for (let i = 0; i < value.length; i++) {
    parts.push(serializeAny(value[i]));
  }
  return `[${parts.join(',')}]`;
}

function serializeAny(value) {
  // This function is polymorphic, but it just dispatches.
  // The actual work functions stay monomorphic.
  switch (typeof value) {
    case 'string': return serializeString(value);
    case 'number': return serializeNumber(value);
    default:
      if (Array.isArray(value)) return serializeArray(value);
      return JSON.stringify(value);
  }
}

// ============================================================
// 6. Verify with V8 flags
// ============================================================

// Check IC states:
// node --trace-ic script.js 2>&1 | grep 'getX'
//
// Output shows:
//   [LoadIC] getX: monomorphic → polymorphic
//   [LoadIC] getX: polymorphic → megamorphic
//
// Check optimization status:
// node --allow-natives-syntax -e "
//   function test(p) { return p.x + p.y; }
//   const p = {x:1, y:2};
//   for(let i=0; i<10000; i++) test(p); // Warm up
//   %OptimizeFunctionOnNextCall(test);
//   test(p);
//   console.log(%GetOptimizationStatus(test));
// "

console.time('Monomorphic');
for (let r = 0; r < 10; r++) monoExample();
console.timeEnd('Monomorphic');

console.time('Megamorphic');
for (let r = 0; r < 10; r++) megaExample();
console.timeEnd('Megamorphic');
```

Key principles: keep objects with the same properties in the same order (uniform shape), avoid mixing shapes in arrays processed by the same function, normalize config objects before processing, and split type-dispatching functions so the inner work functions stay monomorphic. Verify with `node --trace-ic`.

instruction: How do I use ArrayBuffer and typed arrays for high-performance data processing in JavaScript? When do they outperform regular arrays?
output:
<think>
ArrayBuffer and typed arrays (Uint8Array, Float64Array, Int32Array, etc.) provide raw binary data access in JavaScript. They're fundamentally different from regular JS arrays: fixed size, fixed type, contiguous memory, no boxing.

Performance advantages:
1. No type boxing — regular arrays can hold any type, so each element is a tagged value. Typed arrays store raw numeric values without tags.
2. Contiguous memory — cache-friendly sequential access patterns
3. Predictable size — V8 knows the exact memory layout at compile time
4. Zero-copy interop — can share memory with WebAssembly, Web Workers (SharedArrayBuffer), native addons
5. Efficient serialization — can be sent over network/IPC without JSON overhead

When typed arrays win:
- Large numeric datasets (>10K elements)
- Image/audio/video processing
- Binary protocol parsing
- Scientific computation
- Sharing data between workers without copying

When regular arrays are fine:
- Small arrays (<1000 elements)
- Mixed-type data
- Dynamic sizing (push/pop)
- When readability matters more than performance

I should show: creating and using typed arrays, DataView for parsing binary protocols, SharedArrayBuffer for worker communication, and performance comparison.

Important: SharedArrayBuffer requires cross-origin isolation headers (COOP/COEP) in browsers. Also, Atomics API for thread-safe operations on shared memory.
</think>

Here's how to use typed arrays and ArrayBuffer for high-performance JavaScript:

```javascript
// ============================================================
// 1. Typed Array Basics
// ============================================================

// Regular array: each element is a tagged JS value (~16-24 bytes each)
const regularArray = [1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 4.0];

// Float64Array: raw 8-byte doubles, contiguous (exactly 32 bytes)
const typedArray = new Float64Array([1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 4.0]);

// ArrayBuffer: raw binary data (the underlying storage)
const buffer = new ArrayBuffer(1024); // 1KB of raw bytes
const f64View = new Float64Array(buffer); // 128 doubles
const u8View = new Uint8Array(buffer);    // 1024 bytes (same memory!)

// Multiple views on the same buffer (zero-copy type punning)
f64View[0] = 3.14;
console.log(u8View[0]); // See the raw bytes of 3.14

// ============================================================
// 2. Performance comparison: typed vs regular arrays
// ============================================================

function benchmarkSum(label, createArray, N) {
  const arr = createArray(N);
  
  // Warm up
  let sum = 0;
  for (let i = 0; i < arr.length; i++) sum += arr[i];
  
  const start = performance.now();
  const RUNS = 100;
  for (let r = 0; r < RUNS; r++) {
    sum = 0;
    for (let i = 0; i < arr.length; i++) {
      sum += arr[i];
    }
  }
  const elapsed = performance.now() - start;
  console.log(`${label}: ${elapsed.toFixed(1)}ms (${RUNS} runs, sum=${sum.toFixed(2)})`);
}

const N = 1_000_000;
benchmarkSum('Regular Array ', (n) => Array.from({length: n}, (_, i) => i * 0.1), N);
benchmarkSum('Float64Array  ', (n) => Float64Array.from({length: n}, (_, i) => i * 0.1), N);
// Float64Array is typically 2-5x faster for numeric operations

// ============================================================
// 3. Binary protocol parsing with DataView
// ============================================================

function parsePacket(buffer) {
  // Parse a binary network packet:
  // Bytes 0-1: uint16 message type (big-endian)
  // Bytes 2-5: uint32 sequence number
  // Bytes 6-9: float32 payload value
  // Bytes 10+: variable-length UTF-8 string
  
  const view = new DataView(buffer);
  
  const msgType = view.getUint16(0, false);    // big-endian
  const seqNum = view.getUint32(2, false);     // big-endian
  const value = view.getFloat32(6, false);     // big-endian
  
  // Decode UTF-8 string from remaining bytes
  const textBytes = new Uint8Array(buffer, 10);
  const text = new TextDecoder().decode(textBytes);
  
  return { msgType, seqNum, value, text };
}

function buildPacket(msgType, seqNum, value, text) {
  const textBytes = new TextEncoder().encode(text);
  const buffer = new ArrayBuffer(10 + textBytes.length);
  const view = new DataView(buffer);
  
  view.setUint16(0, msgType, false);
  view.setUint32(2, seqNum, false);
  view.setFloat32(6, value, false);
  
  new Uint8Array(buffer, 10).set(textBytes);
  return buffer;
}

// Usage:
const packet = buildPacket(0x0001, 42, 3.14, 'Hello');
const parsed = parsePacket(packet);
console.log(parsed); // { msgType: 1, seqNum: 42, value: 3.14, text: 'Hello' }

// ============================================================
// 4. Image processing with typed arrays
// ============================================================

function adjustBrightness(imageData, factor) {
  // imageData.data is a Uint8ClampedArray (RGBA pixels)
  // Uint8ClampedArray auto-clamps to [0, 255] — no manual bounds checking
  const data = imageData.data;
  
  // Process 4 bytes at a time (RGBA)
  for (let i = 0; i < data.length; i += 4) {
    data[i]     = data[i]     * factor; // R (auto-clamped)
    data[i + 1] = data[i + 1] * factor; // G
    data[i + 2] = data[i + 2] * factor; // B
    // data[i + 3] = alpha, leave unchanged
  }
}

// Even faster: process as Uint32Array (4 pixels = 1 read)
function invertColors(imageData) {
  const u32 = new Uint32Array(imageData.data.buffer);
  for (let i = 0; i < u32.length; i++) {
    // XOR RGB bytes, preserve alpha
    u32[i] = (u32[i] ^ 0x00FFFFFF) | (u32[i] & 0xFF000000);
  }
}

// ============================================================
// 5. SharedArrayBuffer for zero-copy Worker communication
// ============================================================

// Main thread:
function setupSharedComputation() {
  const sharedBuf = new SharedArrayBuffer(Float64Array.BYTES_PER_ELEMENT * 1_000_000);
  const sharedArr = new Float64Array(sharedBuf);
  
  // Initialize data
  for (let i = 0; i < sharedArr.length; i++) {
    sharedArr[i] = Math.random();
  }
  
  // Send to worker — ZERO COPY (shared memory)
  // worker.postMessage({ buffer: sharedBuf, start: 0, end: 500_000 });
  // worker2.postMessage({ buffer: sharedBuf, start: 500_000, end: 1_000_000 });
  
  // Workers can read/write the same memory directly.
  // Use Atomics for synchronization:
  const syncBuf = new SharedArrayBuffer(Int32Array.BYTES_PER_ELEMENT * 2);
  const syncArr = new Int32Array(syncBuf);
  
  // Worker signals completion:
  // Atomics.store(syncArr, 0, 1);
  // Atomics.notify(syncArr, 0);
  
  // Main thread waits:
  // Atomics.wait(syncArr, 0, 0); // Block until value != 0
}

// ============================================================
// 6. Efficient buffer pooling for hot paths
// ============================================================

class TypedArrayPool {
  constructor(TypedArrayClass, initialSize = 1024) {
    this.TypedArrayClass = TypedArrayClass;
    this.pool = [];
    this.defaultSize = initialSize;
  }
  
  acquire(minSize) {
    const size = Math.max(minSize || 0, this.defaultSize);
    
    // Find a pooled buffer that's large enough
    for (let i = 0; i < this.pool.length; i++) {
      if (this.pool[i].length >= size) {
        return this.pool.splice(i, 1)[0];
      }
    }
    
    // Create new if pool empty
    return new this.TypedArrayClass(size);
  }
  
  release(arr) {
    if (this.pool.length < 10) { // Cap pool size
      this.pool.push(arr);
    }
  }
}

const f64Pool = new TypedArrayPool(Float64Array, 4096);

function processWithPool(data) {
  const buf = f64Pool.acquire(data.length);
  try {
    for (let i = 0; i < data.length; i++) {
      buf[i] = Math.sqrt(data[i]);
    }
    // Use buf[0..data.length]
    return buf.subarray(0, data.length); // Zero-copy view
  } finally {
    // Don't release yet — caller has a view!
    // Release after caller is done with the subarray
  }
}
```

Typed arrays win for large numeric datasets, binary protocol handling, image/audio processing, and shared-memory parallelism with Workers. For small arrays or mixed-type data, regular arrays are fine. The key advantage is memory layout: contiguous, fixed-type, cache-friendly, and zero-copy shareable.

instruction: How do I detect and fix memory leaks in a Node.js application? Show practical debugging techniques.
output:
<think>
Node.js memory leaks are tricky because JavaScript's GC usually handles cleanup, so leaks come from unintentional references keeping objects alive. Common causes:

1. Growing event listeners — adding listeners in request handlers without removing them
2. Closures capturing scope — callbacks holding references to large objects
3. Global caches without eviction — Maps/objects that grow forever
4. Timers not cleared — setInterval/setTimeout keeping callbacks alive
5. Streams not properly destroyed — readable streams left open
6. Promise chains — unresolved promises holding onto their closure scope

Debugging tools:
- `--inspect` + Chrome DevTools heap snapshots — the gold standard
- `process.memoryUsage()` — quick check for growing RSS/heapUsed
- Heap snapshot comparison — take two snapshots, diff to find retained objects
- `--expose-gc` + manual GC before snapshots for accuracy
- `clinic.js` — automated leak detection

The workflow should be:
1. Monitor `process.memoryUsage().heapUsed` over time
2. If growing: take heap snapshots before and after load
3. Compare snapshots in Chrome DevTools → "Comparison" view
4. Look at "Retained size" and "Distance" to find what's holding references
5. Trace back to the root cause

I should show practical code patterns for both creating and fixing common leaks, plus the debugging workflow.
</think>

Here are practical techniques for finding and fixing Node.js memory leaks:

```javascript
// ============================================================
// 1. Memory monitoring — detect leaks early
// ============================================================

class MemoryMonitor {
  constructor(intervalMs = 30000) {
    this.samples = [];
    this.interval = setInterval(() => this.sample(), intervalMs);
  }

  sample() {
    const mem = process.memoryUsage();
    this.samples.push({
      time: Date.now(),
      heapUsed: mem.heapUsed,
      heapTotal: mem.heapTotal,
      rss: mem.rss,
      external: mem.external,
    });

    // Keep last 100 samples
    if (this.samples.length > 100) this.samples.shift();

    // Alert if heap is growing consistently
    if (this.samples.length >= 5) {
      const recent = this.samples.slice(-5);
      const allGrowing = recent.every((s, i) =>
        i === 0 || s.heapUsed > recent[i - 1].heapUsed
      );
      if (allGrowing) {
        const growth = recent[4].heapUsed - recent[0].heapUsed;
        console.warn(
          `[LEAK?] Heap grew ${(growth / 1024 / 1024).toFixed(1)}MB ` +
          `over last 5 samples. Current: ${(recent[4].heapUsed / 1024 / 1024).toFixed(1)}MB`
        );
      }
    }
  }

  stop() {
    clearInterval(this.interval);
  }
}

// const monitor = new MemoryMonitor(10000); // Check every 10s

// ============================================================
// 2. Common leak patterns and fixes
// ============================================================

const EventEmitter = require('events');

// LEAK 1: Growing event listeners
class LeakyServer {
  constructor() {
    this.emitter = new EventEmitter();
  }

  handleRequest(req) {
    // BUG: Adds a NEW listener per request — never removed!
    this.emitter.on('update', (data) => {
      // Uses req in closure — req can never be GC'd
      console.log(`Update for ${req.id}: ${data}`);
    });
  }
}

class FixedServer {
  constructor() {
    this.emitter = new EventEmitter();
  }

  handleRequest(req) {
    // FIX 1: Use once() for one-shot listeners
    this.emitter.once('update', (data) => {
      console.log(`Update for ${req.id}: ${data}`);
    });

    // FIX 2: Or track and remove listeners
    const handler = (data) => console.log(`Update: ${data}`);
    this.emitter.on('update', handler);
    // Later (e.g., on request end):
    // this.emitter.off('update', handler);
  }
}

// LEAK 2: Unbounded cache
class LeakyCache {
  constructor() {
    this.cache = new Map(); // Grows forever!
  }

  get(key, computeFn) {
    if (!this.cache.has(key)) {
      this.cache.set(key, computeFn());
    }
    return this.cache.get(key);
  }
}

class LRUCache {
  constructor(maxSize = 1000) {
    this.cache = new Map();
    this.maxSize = maxSize;
  }

  get(key, computeFn) {
    if (this.cache.has(key)) {
      // Move to end (most recently used)
      const value = this.cache.get(key);
      this.cache.delete(key);
      this.cache.set(key, value);
      return value;
    }

    const value = computeFn();
    this.cache.set(key, value);

    // Evict oldest if over capacity
    if (this.cache.size > this.maxSize) {
      const oldestKey = this.cache.keys().next().value;
      this.cache.delete(oldestKey);
    }
    return value;
  }
}

// LEAK 3: Closures capturing large scope
function leakyProcessor() {
  const hugeDataset = Buffer.alloc(100 * 1024 * 1024); // 100MB

  return {
    // This closure captures hugeDataset even though it only needs a small part
    getSummary: () => {
      return { size: hugeDataset.length }; // Holds 100MB alive!
    },
  };
}

function fixedProcessor() {
  const hugeDataset = Buffer.alloc(100 * 1024 * 1024);

  // Extract what you need, then let hugeDataset be GC'd
  const size = hugeDataset.length;
  // hugeDataset goes out of scope here if no other references

  return {
    getSummary: () => ({ size }), // Only captures the number
  };
}

// LEAK 4: Uncleared timers
class LeakyService {
  start() {
    // BUG: setInterval keeps this alive even if nothing references the service
    this.timer = setInterval(() => {
      this.poll(); // 'this' captured → service can't be GC'd
    }, 5000);
  }

  poll() { /* ... */ }

  // FIX: Always provide cleanup
  stop() {
    clearInterval(this.timer);
    this.timer = null;
  }
}

// ============================================================
// 3. Heap snapshot debugging workflow
// ============================================================

// Step 1: Start app with --inspect
// node --inspect --expose-gc app.js

// Step 2: Open Chrome DevTools (chrome://inspect)
// Step 3: Go to Memory tab

// Programmatic heap snapshots (for automated debugging):
const v8 = require('v8');
const fs = require('fs');

function takeHeapSnapshot(filename) {
  const snapshotStream = v8.writeHeapSnapshot(filename);
  console.log(`Heap snapshot written to: ${snapshotStream}`);
  return snapshotStream;
}

// Automated leak detection workflow:
async function detectLeak(workloadFn, iterations = 100) {
  // Force GC for clean baseline
  if (global.gc) global.gc();
  const baseline = process.memoryUsage().heapUsed;

  // Run workload
  for (let i = 0; i < iterations; i++) {
    await workloadFn();
  }

  // Force GC and measure
  if (global.gc) global.gc();
  const after = process.memoryUsage().heapUsed;

  const growth = after - baseline;
  const perIteration = growth / iterations;

  console.log(`Memory growth: ${(growth / 1024).toFixed(1)} KB total`);
  console.log(`Per iteration: ${(perIteration / 1024).toFixed(2)} KB`);

  if (perIteration > 1024) { // > 1KB per iteration
    console.warn('PROBABLE LEAK: taking heap snapshots...');
    takeHeapSnapshot('heap-leak.heapsnapshot');
    // Load this in Chrome DevTools → Memory → Load
  }

  return { growth, perIteration };
}

// Usage:
// detectLeak(async () => {
//   const res = await fetch('/api/data');
//   await res.json();
// });

// ============================================================
// 4. WeakRef + FinalizationRegistry for leak tracking
// ============================================================

class LeakTracker {
  constructor() {
    this.tracked = new Map(); // id → WeakRef
    this.registry = new FinalizationRegistry((id) => {
      this.tracked.delete(id);
      // Object was GC'd — not a leak
    });
    this.nextId = 0;
  }

  track(obj, label) {
    const id = this.nextId++;
    this.tracked.set(id, { ref: new WeakRef(obj), label, created: Date.now() });
    this.registry.register(obj, id);
    return id;
  }

  report() {
    const alive = [];
    for (const [id, entry] of this.tracked) {
      const obj = entry.ref.deref();
      if (obj !== undefined) {
        const age = Date.now() - entry.created;
        alive.push({ id, label: entry.label, ageMs: age });
      }
    }
    console.log(`Tracked objects still alive: ${alive.length}`);
    alive.sort((a, b) => b.ageMs - a.ageMs);
    alive.slice(0, 10).forEach(e =>
      console.log(`  [${e.id}] ${e.label} — alive for ${(e.ageMs / 1000).toFixed(0)}s`)
    );
  }
}

// const tracker = new LeakTracker();
// In request handler:
// const data = loadData();
// tracker.track(data, 'request-data');
// Periodically:
// tracker.report();  // Shows objects that should have been GC'd but weren't

console.log('Run with: node --inspect --expose-gc script.js');
console.log('Then open chrome://inspect for heap snapshots');
```

Debugging workflow: (1) monitor `process.memoryUsage()` to detect growth, (2) take heap snapshots before/after load with `--expose-gc`, (3) compare snapshots in Chrome DevTools to find retained objects, (4) trace retainer chains to the root cause. Most leaks are event listeners, unbounded caches, or closures capturing large scope.
